attributes.stem:
A 30-year-old man presents to the GP wanting to address his chronic lower back pain that has been negatively impacting his sleep. After asking further about his history, you find out that he has had lower back pain for the past year that radiates to his both his buttocks and sometimes wakes him up in the middle of the night. He also has back stiffness in the mornings for approximately an hour before he takes a hot shower, and the pain is relieved. He says his father had similar issues with back pain and stiffness.

On examination, there is decreased range of motion on flexion of the lumbar spine due to stiffness and slight pain. His sacroiliac joints are also tender to palpation bilaterally. An X-ray of the lumbar spine showed loss of bone density and bilateral sclerosis of the sacroiliac joints.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which of the following findings is most commonly associated with this patient's condition?
attributes.explanation:
Red and painful eye is the correct answer. This patient most likely has ankylosing spondylitis, a seronegative spondyloarthropathy which is associated with multiple extra-articular manifestations. Acute anterior uveitis is the most common extra-articular manifestation (~25%), and typical symptoms include eye pain, redness, constricted and fixed pupil, as well as decreased visual acuity. On clinical exam, ankylosing spondylitis is characterised by progressive lower back pain with sacroiliac joint tenderness on palpation, together with reduced spinal mobility and morning stiffness lasting more than 30 minutes that improves with physical activity. Other than joint issues, ankylosing spondylitis can also affect the gastrointestinal tract (inflammatory bowel disease), lungs (pulmonary fibrosis), and the heart (aortitis, aortic valve insufficiency).

Fever and tachycardia are systemic symptoms that are usually present in infection, which may be present in osteomyelitis leading to sepsis. Although vertebral osteomyelitis can manifest with acute neck pain, this patient does not have any risk factors for osteomyelitis such as an open wound, intravenous drug use, or any signs of a pre-existing primary infection. Furthermore, the onset of pain in osteomyelitis is insidious in onset and severe in nature, whereas the patient has chronic back pain.

Hand stiffness and deformities are uncommon presentations in ankylosing spondylitis, but dactylitis (sausage-digit) can be one of the articular manifestations. Hand involvement (peripheral involvement) is usually linked to rheumatoid arthritis (RA), which can cause inflammation, swelling, and stiffness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. It can also result in deformities in the hand such as swan neck deformity and Boutonniere deformity. Although rheumatoid arthritis can affect multiple joints such as the wrist, knees, and ankles, the disease usually spares the thoracic and lumbar spine. In addition, RA can also cause erosions at the atlantoaxial joint, and sacroiliac joint involvement is rare.

Constipation and muscle weakness are symptoms of hypercalcaemia, which is linked to multiple myeloma. It is characterised by abnormal proliferation of plasma cells with infiltration into the bone marrow, and typically presents with B symptoms, back/bone pain, pathological spine fractures, and foamy urine (Bence Jones protein). X-ray imaging (skeletal survey) would show "punched-out" lytic lesions especially in the skull, and multiple myeloma is confirmed with a bone marrow biopsy. The acronym 'CRAB' is used in relation to multiple myeloma, representing: Calcium elevated, Renal insufficiency, Anaemia, and Bone lesions.

Urethritis is a symptom commonly associated with reactive arthritis, a condition within the spectrum of seronegative spondyloarthropathies. It classically presents with inflammatory pain that is migratory in nature, usually asymmetric and involving the lower extremities. This condition is typically precipitated by a gastrointestinal or urinary tract infection, with pathogens such as Chlamydia, Shigella, Salmonella, and Campylobacter commonly found responsible. Although reactive arthritis can also cause sacroiliitis, it is usually unilateral instead of bilateral. A common phrase associated with reactive arthritis is "Can't see, can't pee, can't climb a tree" which represents the triad of urethritis, conjunctivitis, and arthritis involved in this condition.
learningPoints: Characteristic x-ray findings in ankylosing spondylitis - Sacroiliitis, bamboo spine, and dagger sign
90% of individuals with ankylosing spondylitis are positive for HLA-B27, but only 1% of individuals who are positive for HLA-B27 have ankylosing spondylitis
references: Ebrahimiadib N, Berijani S, Ghahari M, Pahlaviani FG. Ankylosing spondylitis. Journal of Ophthalmic & Vision Research. 2021 Jul;16(3):462.
Sen R, Goyal A, Bansal P, Hurley JA. Seronegative spondyloarthropathy.
